questions.title: Animal Moral Weights
questions.heading: How much should animal welfare factor into funding decisions?
questions.info: Moral weights determine how much you value improvements to different types of beings. Human life-years are the baseline (1.0). Your answer here affects how the model scores funds that focus on animal welfare.

options.label: Only humans matter
options.description: Animal welfare isn't considered. Funding decisions are based solely on benefits to people.

options.label: Animals matter, but much less than humans
options.description: Animal welfare counts, but is given substantially lower weight than human welfare.

options.label: Animals matter, but less than humans
options.description: Animal welfare is given meaningful consideration though not weighted equally with human welfare.

options.label: All sentient beings matter equally
options.description: A year of suffering matters the same whether experienced by a human or an animal. No species is prioritized over another.

questions.title: Time Discounting
questions.heading: How much should future benefits and harms count compared to those happening now?
questions.info:
You might have reasons to discount effects that happen in the future. For example, the further out an effect is, the greater the chance that something intervenes and the estimated effect never occurs.

The model divides effects into six time periods: 0–5 years, 5–10 years, 10–20 years, 20–100 years, 100–500 years, and 500+ years. Each option sets a multiplier for each period. A multiplier of 0 means effects in that period are ignored entirely; 1 means they count fully.

options.label: Near term only
options.description: Only effects in the next decade matter. Everything beyond that is ignored.

options.label: Mid-term only
options.description: Only the next century matters, with more weight on the near term. Effects beyond 100 years are ignored.

options.label: All time periods matter, near term gets more weight
options.description: Near-term effects count fully. Effects further out are progressively discounted, with distant effects counting very little.

options.label: All time periods count equally
options.description: Benefits and harms are valued equally, whether they occur tomorrow or a century from now.

questions.title: Non-AI X-Risk Discount
questions.heading: How much priority should reducing existential risk from AI receive over other causes?
questions.info: This adjustment applies only to interventions that do not focus on reducing existential risk from AI. Global health and development, animal welfare, biosecurity, and nuclear risk interventions are reduced in value. The value is a discount fraction: 0.0 means no discount, 0.4 means a 40% discount, 1.0 means that all non-AI interventions score zero.

options.label: No extra priority
options.description: Work on AI risk is evaluated the same way as all other interventions.

options.label: Slight priority for AI risk (10% discount)
options.description: Other interventions are scored at 90% of their calculated value.

options.label: Strong priority for AI risk (40% discount)
options.description: Other interventions are scored at 60% of their calculated value.

options.label: Very strong priority for AI risk (80% discount)
options.description: Other interventions are scored at 20% of their calculated value.

questions.title: Risk Attitude
questions.heading: How do you want to handle uncertainty and risk when comparing funds?
questions.info: Each fund's impact is estimated as an expected value across a range of scenarios with different probabilities. The risk profile determines how those scenarios are weighted. A risk-neutral approach takes expected value at face value. Other options adjust for uncertainty by discounting optimistic outliers, putting extra weight on potential harms, or both.

options.label: Risk neutral
options.description: Take expected value estimates at face value. If something has a 10% chance of saving 1,000 lives, count it as 100 expected lives saved. No adjustment for uncertainty or risk.

options.label: Skeptical of very large claimed benefits
options.description: Reduces the contribution of the most optimistic impact scenarios on the score. Funds whose scores depend heavily on low-probability, very large outcomes will score lower.

options.label: Put extra weight on avoiding harms
options.description: Applies extra weight to scenarios where an intervention backfires or causes harm. Funds with significant downside risk will score lower even if their expected value is high.

options.label: Stake-sensitive
options.description: Reduces the weight of large benefits and increases the weight of large harms relative to the standard expected value.
